title: Conductivity and Permeability Effects
narrationScript: Every eddy current signal is the coil reporting on the material beneath it, and two material properties do most of the talking. The first is conductivity, how freely electrons flow. We grade it as a percentage of the international annealed copper standard, where copper reads one hundred percent. Silver runs about one hundred five. Aerospace aluminum alloys land near thirty. Titanium and austenitic stainless sit near three. Raise the conductivity and the eddy currents grow stronger, the operating point slides farther around the comma shaped conductivity curve, and the depth of penetration shrinks. Copper at one hundred kilohertz tests only about two tenths of a millimeter deep. Permeability is the second property, and when it appears, it wins. Ferromagnetic steel multiplies the coil's flux, drives inductive reactance above the air point, collapses penetration to a thin surface skin, and adds noise wherever permeability wanders with stress or microstructure. The cure is magnetic saturation, which forces steel to behave like a nonmagnetic conductor. Master these two properties and you can sort alloys, verify heat treatment, and catch hidden fire damage.

sections.heading: Two Material Properties Own the Signal
sections.body:
By now you can explain where eddy currents come from: the coil's alternating magnetic field induces circulating currents in any nearby conductor, and those currents push back on the coil. Frequency, you have seen, is the operator's knob — it sets phase and depth of penetration. This lesson is about the two knobs you do not control, because the part owns them:

- **Electrical conductivity (σ)** — how freely the material lets electrons flow.
- **Magnetic permeability (μ)** — how strongly the material concentrates magnetic flux.

Between them, these two properties decide how strong the eddy currents are, how deep they reach, and which direction the signal moves on the impedance plane. Same probe, same frequency, different alloy — completely different response.

That sensitivity is a capability, not a nuisance. Because the method reads σ and μ directly, eddy current testing can sort mixed alloys, verify heat treatment, and find fire-damaged aircraft structure — jobs no other surface NDT method does. It also means an unnoticed material change can masquerade as a flaw, which is why a Level I must know exactly what each property does to the signal.

sections.heading: Conductivity and the IACS Scale
sections.body:
**Conductivity** measures how easily current flows; it is the reciprocal of resistivity. The SI unit is siemens per meter, but eddy current work uses a more convenient yardstick: **% IACS**, the International Annealed Copper Standard. By definition, **100% IACS is the conductivity of annealed, commercially pure copper at 20 °C** (resistivity 1.7241 µΩ·cm, about 58 MS/m). Every other material is graded against copper — and the scale is not capped: silver beats the standard at about 105%, while titanium limps in near 3%.

Conductivity is not a fixed label stamped on an alloy. Four things move it:

- **Alloying.** Atoms added to a pure metal scatter electrons. Pure aluminum runs about 61% IACS; alloyed and hardened 2024-T4 drops to about 30%.
- **Heat treatment and temper.** Solution treating, aging, and annealing change how much alloying element is dissolved in the lattice, so conductivity tracks temper — the basis for heat-treat verification.
- **Temperature.** Warmer metal vibrates more and conducts less, so readings drift with temperature. Part, probe, and reference standards must be at the same temperature.
- **Cold work and residual stress.** Plastic deformation adds lattice defects that scatter electrons — a smaller effect, but real.

Hardness, strength, and conductivity all ride on the same microstructure. That is why a conductivity meter can stand in as a strength screen on aluminum.

sections.caption: The IACS conductivity ladder for common metals. Copper defines 100%; aerospace aluminum alloys cluster near 30%; titanium, austenitic stainless, and nickel alloys sit in the low single digits. Steel's ~10% is almost irrelevant — its ferromagnetic permeability dominates the response.

sections.heading: What Conductivity Does to the Signal
sections.body:
Put the same coil, at the same frequency, on a series of nonmagnetic metals and watch the impedance plane.

**Signal strength.** Higher conductivity lets the induced voltage drive more current, so the eddy currents — and the secondary field they throw back at the coil — grow stronger. The coil's impedance changes more: the operating point travels farther from the **air point**, sweeping down and around the comma-shaped **conductivity curve**. Low-conductivity metals (titanium, austenitic stainless) cluster near the air point; high-conductivity metals (copper, silver) ride at the far end. The spacing between alloys along that curve is real, repeatable information — it is exactly what a conductivity meter reads.

**Depth of penetration.** Conductivity sits in the denominator of the standard-depth formula you met last lesson, δ = 1/√(πfμσ). Stronger eddy currents shield the interior more effectively, so **the better the conductor, the shallower the test**. At 100 kHz, one standard depth is about **0.2 mm in copper** but about **1.2 mm in titanium** — same coil, same frequency, nearly a six-fold difference owned entirely by the material.

The practical consequence: you cannot choose a test frequency until you know what the part is made of. Material conductivity is the first line on any eddy current technique sheet.

sections.caption: Approximate room-temperature conductivity and magnetic class of materials you will meet on the bench.
rows: Strongest eddy currents — far end of the conductivity curve
Defines the IACS scale; very shallow penetration
Alloying away from purity drops the reading fast
Aerospace structure — temper and heat damage shift the reading
Weak eddy currents, deep penetration — plots near the air point
Nonmagnetic (can turn slightly magnetic when heavily cold-worked)
Low conductivity; watch for cold-work permeability surprises
Permeability dominates — reactance climbs above the air point

sections.heading: Permeability: The Flux Multiplier
sections.body:
**Magnetic permeability (μ)** is a material's flux-multiplying ability — the flux density produced per unit of magnetizing force (μ = B/H). We normalize it to free space as **relative permeability, μr**. Copper, aluminum, titanium, and annealed austenitic stainless are **nonmagnetic: μr ≈ 1**, so permeability quietly drops out of their behavior.

**Ferromagnetic** materials — iron, nickel, cobalt, carbon and low-alloy steels, 400-series stainless — are another world. Their **μr runs from the hundreds into the thousands**, and it is not even constant: it changes with magnetizing force, heat treatment, stress, and the part's magnetic history. Three consequences hit the test at once:

- **Reactance climbs.** The part concentrates the coil's flux, so coil inductance — and inductive reactance — jumps. The operating point moves **above the air point**: the opposite direction from conductivity loading, and so much larger that conductivity information is buried.
- **Penetration collapses.** μ sits under the same square root as σ in δ = 1/√(πfμσ). Where 7075 aluminum gives you roughly 0.4 mm at 100 kHz, mild steel gives **well under 0.1 mm** — on ferromagnetic parts, eddy current testing is a surface examination.
- **Permeability noise.** μr wanders from spot to spot with stress and microstructure, producing baseline signals that can dwarf a real crack indication.

The standard cure is **magnetic saturation**: a strong DC field drives the steel onto the flat top of its B-H curve, where the incremental permeability falls toward 1. Saturated, the part behaves like a nonmagnetic conductor — stable signals, predictable penetration. That is exactly how ferromagnetic tubing is examined in practice. One more escape hatch exists: above its **Curie temperature** (about 770 °C for iron), steel loses its ferromagnetism entirely, which is how hot-rolled steel can be tested in the mill.

sections.caption: The impedance plane. Nonmagnetic conductors leave the air point and slide around the comma-shaped conductivity curve — farther as σ·f rises. A ferromagnetic part moves the opposite way: permeability multiplies the flux and drives the operating point above the air point. Lift-off always pulls back toward the air point.

sections.title: Reading the plane in one breath
sections.body: Nonmagnetic conductor: the operating point leaves the air point and slides **down around the comma curve** — the higher the conductivity (and frequency), the farther it travels. Ferromagnetic part: the point **climbs above the air point** — permeability wins, every time. Lift-off: always **back toward the air point**. Field habit: touch a pocket magnet to the part before you test. If it sticks, conductivity readings are off the table, and the technique must deal with permeability — usually by magnetic saturation.

sections.heading: Field Use: Sorting, Heat Treat, and Heat Damage
sections.body:
Level I eddy current work leans on conductivity every week:

- **Conductivity measurement.** Dedicated meters — governed by **ASTM E1004** — read % IACS directly, commonly operating near 60 kHz (higher frequencies for thin stock). The discipline: verify on two standards that bracket the expected value, keep probe, part, and standards at the same temperature, and test on a flat, clean, accessible surface. Thin nonconductive coatings are tolerated only within the limits of your procedure, because lift-off compensation has limits.
- **Alloy sorting.** Mixed stock with different conductivities separates in seconds — 2024 near 30% IACS will never read like 6061-T6 in the low-to-mid 40s.
- **Heat-treat verification.** Temper changes conductivity, so aerospace procedures verify aluminum heat treatment by conductivity, often paired with hardness testing.
- **Heat and fire damage.** 7075-T6 should read about **30.5–33% IACS**. Overheat it — engine fire, lightning strike, a hangar fire — and the temper overages toward the annealed condition: conductivity climbs toward the **mid-40s** while strength falls. A conductivity map flags damaged skin that looks perfectly sound to the eye.

On ferromagnetic parts those readings mean nothing — permeability swamps them. Know which regime you are standing in before you trust a number; that is the heart of this lesson.

figures.title: IACS conductivity scale for common metals
Impedance plane: conductivity curve and the permeability direction

interactive.title: Where Does Steel Land?
interactive.intro: An absolute probe is balanced in air, then placed on a mild-steel fitting. Using the impedance plane from this lesson, click the region where the operating point lands.

regions.label: Above the air point
regions.feedback: Correct. Ferromagnetic permeability multiplies the coil's flux, so inductive reactance climbs above the air point — the opposite direction from conductivity loading. The μ effect is so large it buries conductivity information until the part is magnetically saturated.

regions.label: The air point
regions.feedback: That is where the point starts, not where it lands. The air point is the balanced, no-part condition; touching the probe to any conductive or magnetic material moves the point away from it.

regions.label: Low-conductivity end of the curve (Ti / 304 SS)
regions.feedback: This is where low-conductivity NONMAGNETIC metals land. Steel conducts about as poorly (≈10% IACS), but its permeability overrides conductivity and drives the operating point upward instead of along the curve.

regions.label: High-conductivity end of the curve (Al / Cu)
regions.feedback: Copper and aluminum live here — strong eddy currents with μr ≈ 1. Steel's eddy currents are comparatively weak, and permeability, not conductivity, controls where it plots.

checkQuestions.question: On the IACS scale used by every conductivity meter, 100% IACS is defined as the conductivity of:
options: Pure silver, the best electrical conductor
Annealed, commercially pure copper at 20 °C
Electrolytically refined aluminum
Annealed low-carbon steel
checkQuestions.explanation: IACS stands for International Annealed Copper Standard: annealed pure copper at 20 °C (resistivity 1.7241 µΩ·cm) defines 100%. The scale is not capped there — silver reads about 105% IACS.

checkQuestions.question: Same coil, same frequency: you move the probe from titanium (≈3% IACS) to copper (100% IACS). The standard depth of penetration:
options: Increases, because better conductors carry current deeper
Stays the same, because depth depends only on frequency
Decreases, because stronger eddy currents shield the interior and crowd the current toward the surface
Becomes effectively infinite in a near-perfect conductor
checkQuestions.explanation: Conductivity sits in the denominator of δ = 1/√(πfμσ). At 100 kHz one standard depth is roughly 1.2 mm in titanium but only about 0.2 mm in copper — higher conductivity always means a shallower test.

checkQuestions.question: An absolute coil balanced in air is placed on a mild-steel part. Relative to the air point, the operating point:
options: Climbs above it, because permeability multiplies the coil's flux and raises inductive reactance
Slides to the far, high-conductivity end of the comma curve
Barely moves, because steel is a poor conductor
Moves only along the resistance axis
checkQuestions.explanation: Ferromagnetic μr — hundreds or more — concentrates flux and drives coil reactance up, the opposite direction from conductivity loading, and so strongly that conductivity information is buried beneath it.

checkQuestions.question: Steel tubing is eddy current examined under a strong DC magnetic saturation field primarily to:
options: Raise the tube's conductivity so eddy currents flow more strongly
Attract magnetic particles to surface cracks
Demagnetize the tube after the examination
Drive the effective permeability toward 1, suppressing permeability noise and restoring predictable penetration
checkQuestions.explanation: Saturation pushes the steel onto the flat region of its B-H curve, where incremental μr approaches 1. The tube then behaves like a nonmagnetic conductor — stable signals and usable penetration. ASTM E309 practice is built on this.

checkQuestions.question: A 7075-T6 skin panel nominally reads 30.5–33% IACS. After a hangar fire, one zone reads 41% IACS. The most likely interpretation is:
options: Instrument drift — 7075 cannot read above 33% IACS
Fire-induced cold work raised the conductivity
Heat exposure overaged or annealed the temper — conductivity moved toward the annealed value and strength has been lost
The zone has become ferromagnetic
checkQuestions.explanation: Overheating drives 7075-T6 toward the annealed condition, whose conductivity sits in the mid-40s % IACS. Rising conductivity with falling strength is exactly the signature conductivity mapping is used to find — the metal can look completely undamaged.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, ECT Level I Theory Course §2.1.4.7–2.1.4.8 (electrical effects — conductivity of materials; magnetic effects — permeability of materials)
ASTM E1004, Standard Test Method for Determining Electrical Conductivity Using the Electromagnetic (Eddy Current) Method
ASTM E309, Standard Practice for Eddy-Current Examination of Steel Tubular Products Using Magnetic Saturation
ASM Handbook, Volume 17, Nondestructive Evaluation of Materials (published % IACS conductivity values for common metals and alloys)
